questions.questionText: Type I hypersensitivity (immediate/anaphylactic) is mediated by:
questions.optionB: IgE antibodies bound to mast cells and basophils
questions.explanation: Type I: IgE-mediated. Allergen crosslinks IgE on mast cells/basophils → degranulation → release of histamine, leukotrienes, prostaglandins. Immediate phase (minutes): vasodilation, bronchospasm, edema. Late phase (2-24 hours): eosinophil/neutrophil infiltration. Examples: anaphylaxis, asthma, allergic rhinitis, urticaria, food allergy. Treatment: epinephrine (anaphylaxis), antihistamines, corticosteroids. Omalizumab: anti-IgE antibody. Reference: Robbins — Type I Hypersensitivity.

questions.questionText: Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) is characterized by:
questions.optionB: Anti-nuclear antibodies (ANA), anti-dsDNA antibodies, immune complex deposition → multi-organ involvement
questions.explanation: SLE: prototype autoimmune disease, Type III hypersensitivity (immune complex deposition). ANA: most sensitive (>95%). Anti-dsDNA: most specific, correlates with disease activity and lupus nephritis. Anti-Smith: also highly specific. Multi-organ: kidney (lupus nephritis — Class IV diffuse proliferative is most severe), skin (butterfly rash, discoid), joints (non-erosive arthritis), serositis, hematologic (cytopenias), CNS. Young females (F:M = 9:1). Reference: Robbins — SLE.

questions.optionB: Thymic hypoplasia (22q11.2 deletion) → T cell deficiency
questions.explanation: DiGeorge syndrome: 22q11.2 microdeletion → failure of 3rd and 4th pharyngeal pouch development → thymic hypoplasia/aplasia (T cell deficiency), parathyroid hypoplasia (hypocalcemia/tetany), cardiac defects (conotruncal — tetralogy of Fallot, truncus arteriosus), facial abnormalities. CATCH-22 mnemonic. T cell deficiency → susceptibility to viral/fungal infections. B cells and immunoglobulins may be normal or reduced. FISH for 22q11.2 deletion. Reference: Robbins — DiGeorge.

questions.optionB: Immune complex (antigen-antibody) deposition in tissues → complement activation → inflammation
questions.explanation: Type III: immune complex deposition. Ag-Ab complexes form → deposit in blood vessels, glomeruli, joints → activate complement (C3a, C5a) and recruit neutrophils → tissue damage. Examples: SLE (lupus nephritis), post-streptococcal GN, serum sickness, Arthus reaction (localized), polyarteritis nodosa (Hep B), farmer's lung (type III + IV). Serum sickness: 7-10 days after exposure → fever, urticaria, arthralgia, lymphadenopathy. Reference: Robbins — Type III.

questions.optionB: CD4+ T helper cells (via gp120 binding to CD4 receptor + CCR5/CXCR4 coreceptors)
questions.explanation: HIV: retrovirus (ssRNA, reverse transcriptase). gp120 binds CD4 → gp41 mediates fusion. Coreceptors: CCR5 (macrophage-tropic, early infection) and CXCR4 (T cell-tropic, late). Progressive CD4+ T cell depletion → immunodeficiency → opportunistic infections (PCP, CMV, Candida, Toxoplasma, Cryptococcus, MAC, Kaposi sarcoma). AIDS: CD4 <200/μL or AIDS-defining illness. Diagnosis: ELISA screening → Western blot confirmation (now 4th gen Ag/Ab). HAART: combination antiretrovirals. Reference: Robbins — HIV.

questions.questionText: Organ transplant rejection mediated by preformed antibodies causing immediate graft destruction is:
questions.explanation: Hyperacute rejection: preformed antibodies against donor antigens (anti-ABO or anti-HLA) → immediate (minutes-hours) complement activation → thrombosis, vessel necrosis → graft destruction. Prevented by ABO matching and crossmatch testing. Acute rejection (days-months): T cell-mediated (cellular) and/or antibody-mediated (humoral) → reversible with immunosuppression. Chronic rejection (months-years): fibrosis, vascular intimal thickening → irreversible. GVHD: donor T cells attack recipient (BMT). Reference: Robbins — Transplant Rejection.

questions.optionB: Btk (Bruton tyrosine kinase) mutation → B cell maturation arrest (no mature B cells or plasma cells)
questions.explanation: Bruton's agammaglobulinemia: X-linked, Btk mutation → pre-B cells cannot mature → absence of mature B cells, plasma cells, and ALL immunoglobulin classes (agammaglobulinemia). Presents after 6 months (maternal IgG wanes). Recurrent sinopulmonary infections with encapsulated bacteria (S. pneumoniae, H. influenzae). Normal T cell function. No germinal centers or tonsillar tissue. Live vaccines contraindicated. Treatment: IV immunoglobulin (IVIG). Reference: Robbins — Bruton's.

questions.questionText: Goodpasture syndrome is an example of Type II hypersensitivity targeting:
questions.optionB: Glomerular and pulmonary basement membranes (anti-GBM antibodies against type IV collagen)
questions.explanation: Goodpasture syndrome (anti-GBM disease): Type II hypersensitivity — IgG antibodies against α3 chain of type IV collagen in glomerular and alveolar basement membranes → rapidly progressive (crescentic) glomerulonephritis + pulmonary hemorrhage. Linear IgG deposition on immunofluorescence (pathognomonic). Young males, may present with hemoptysis and hematuria. Treatment: plasmapheresis (remove antibodies), immunosuppression. Reference: Robbins — Goodpasture.

questions.questionText: Common variable immunodeficiency (CVID) is characterized by:
questions.optionB: Defective B cell differentiation → low immunoglobulins (all classes) with normal B cell numbers
questions.explanation: CVID: most common symptomatic primary immunodeficiency in adults. B cells are present but fail to differentiate into plasma cells → hypogammaglobulinemia (all classes). Heterogeneous etiology. Presents in 2nd-3rd decade: recurrent sinopulmonary bacterial infections, increased risk of autoimmune diseases (ITP, autoimmune hemolytic anemia), GI disease, and lymphoma. Normal T cells (usually). Treatment: IVIG. Unlike Bruton's: B cells are present, affects both sexes. Reference: Robbins — CVID.

questions.questionText: Chronic granulomatous disease (CGD) is caused by deficiency of:
questions.explanation: CGD: defective NADPH oxidase → cannot generate superoxide (O2−) and other ROS → impaired intracellular killing of catalase-positive organisms (S. aureus, Aspergillus, Serratia, Nocardia, Burkholderia — can destroy their own H2O2). Most common: X-linked (gp91phox mutation, ~65%). Granuloma formation (chronic inflammation as compensation). Diagnosis: dihydrorhodamine (DHR) flow cytometry or nitroblue tetrazolium (NBT) test (negative in CGD). Prophylaxis: TMP-SMX, itraconazole, IFN-γ. Reference: Robbins — CGD.
